coredata.dc:title: Engineered gadolinium-doped carbon dots for magnetic resonance imaging-guided radiotherapy of tumors
coredata.dc:creator: Du, Fengyi
Zhang, Lirong
Zhang, Li
Zhang, Miaomiao
Gong, Aihua
Tan, Youwen
Miao, Jiawen
Gong, Yuhua
Sun, Mingzhong
Ju, Huixiang
Wu, Chaoyang
Zou, Shenqiang
coredata.dc:description:
The effectiveness of radiotherapy can decrease due to inaccurate positioning of machinery and inherent radioresistance of tumors. To address this issue, we present a novel theranostic nanoplatform based on gadolinium-doped carbon dots (Gd-doped CDs) designed specifically for magnetic resonance imaging (MRI)-guided radiotherapy of tumors. The Gd-doped CDs (∼18 nm) with dispersibility in water and stable photoluminescence were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal approach. After tail vein injection of the Gd-doped CDs, they exhibited a relatively long circulation time (∼6 h), enabled efficient passive tumor targeting. Gd-doped CDs accumulate in the kidney and could be cleared out of the body from bladder. Importantly, they exhibited favorable biocompatibility with excellent performance in longitudinal relaxivity rate (r
1 ) of 6.45 mM−1S−1 and radiosensitization enhancements. These results show that Gd-doped CDs are excellent T 1 contrast agents and radiosensitizers, possessing great promise for MRI-guided radiotherapy of tumors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MRI
Contrast agents
Gadolinium
Carbon dots
Radiotherapy

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Biomaterials at the Nanoscale for Diagnostic Systems

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Biodistribution of Gd-doped CDs in vivo
Evaluation of radiosensitizing Gd-doped CDs in vitro
MR imaging of tumors in vivo
Radiosensitization assessment in vitro and in vivo

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 81301316, 31200676), Ph.D. Programs Foundation of Ministry of Education of China (No. 20123227120008), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2013M540425, 2014T70487, 2015M571705), Senior Talents Scientific Research Foundation of Jiangsu University (No. 13JDG022, 11JDG113), and Health and family planning commission of jiangsu province scientific research subject (H201557).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The characterization of Gd-doped CDs. (A) Low and (B) high magnification TEM images. (C) XRD patterns and (D) The FTIR spectrum of Gd-doped CDs.
(A) The full-scan XPS spectrum of Gd-doped CDs. (B) Gd4d spectrum. (C) C1S spectrum. (D) O1S spectrum.

The optical properties of the Gd-doped CDs. (A) UV-Vis absorbance spectra of the Gd-doped CDs, respectively. The inset of (A) shows a diluted aqueous solution containing the as-prepared Gd-doped CDs. (B) The Tyndall effect exhibited by the Gd-doped CDs in aqueous solution. (C and D) The photoluminescence emission and normalized spectra of the Gd-doped CDs. The PL emission spectra show the results of using progressively longer excitation wavelengths, from 330 nm to 400 nm in 10 nm increments.

(A) Effect of Gd-doped CDs on the viability of HepG2 and VSMC cells using the MTS assay. (B) Laser scanning confocal microscopy images of 4T1 and A549 cells stained with PI and Gd-doped CDs. Scale bars = 50 μm. (C) Hemolytical activity of the Gd-doped CDs at different concentrations (50, 100, 200, 300 and 400 μg/mL). PBS and water were used as negative and positive control, respectively.

MR contrast ability of Gd-doped CDs. (a) T1-weight phantom images of Gd-doped CDs (top) and Magnevist (bottom) at different Gd concentration using a 3T MR scanner. (b) Linear correlation between r1 (T 1 −1) and Gd concentration, based on readings from a. The r1 relaxivity, which is the slope of the curve, was determined to be 6.45 mM−1s−1.

In vivo MR imaging of Gd-doped CDs. (A) T 1-weighted MR images of Gd-DTPA and Gd-doped CDs acquired at different time points after tail vein injection. (B) Quantification of signal changes (SNR ratio) in kidney, bladder, and liver at different time points after administration (n = 3).

T 1-weighted MR imaging and quantiﬁcational analysis of Heps mouse liver cancer xenograft tumors after intravenous injection of Gd-doped CDs nanoparticles. (A and C) T 1-weighted MR images and corresponding quantiﬁcational analysis of mice bearing subcutaneous tumors (colored area) after intravenous injection of Gd-doped CDs nanoparticles (10 mg/kg). The MR image before injection was acquired for comparison. (B and D) T 1-weighted MR images and corresponding quantiﬁcational analysis of mice bearing tumors in situ (white arrows) after intravenous injection of Gd-doped CDs nanoparticles. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Histopathological and immunohistochemical staining analyses for the detection of subcutaneous tumor and tumor in situ. (B) The H&E images of major organs after tail intravenous injections of Gd-doped CDs. (C) Biodistribution of Gd in the major organs of the Herps tumor-bearing mice at 24 h post intravenous injection of Gd-doped CDs.

In vivo evaluation of the efficacy of radiosensitivity of Gd-doped CDs in mice bearing Herps tumors. (A) Tumors growth curves following the different treatment groups, including the saline solution (control), single Gd-doped CDs (G only), single radiation (R only) and Gd-doped CDs combined with radiation (G + R) during a week. (B) Kaplan-Meier survival analysis. The asterisk indicates statistical significance (p < 0.05).
Schematic synthesis of Gd-doped CDs from the microwave-assisted hydrothermal of GDPA and glycine and following applications.

head.ce:title: Engineered gadolinium-doped carbon dots for magnetic resonance imaging-guided radiotherapy of tumors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Hepatosis, The Third Hospital of Zhenjiang Affiliated to Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Third Hospital of Zhenjiang Affiliated to Jiangsu University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Radiology, Affiliated Hospital of Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Oncology, The Affiliated People's Hospital, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212002, Jiangsu, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Clinical Laboratory, Affiliated Yancheng Hospital, School of Medicine, Southeast University, Yancheng, Jiangsu, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The effectiveness of radiotherapy can decrease due to inaccurate positioning of machinery and inherent radioresistance of tumors. To address this issue, we present a novel theranostic nanoplatform based on gadolinium-doped carbon dots (Gd-doped CDs) designed specifically for magnetic resonance imaging (MRI)-guided radiotherapy of tumors. The Gd-doped CDs (∼18 nm) with dispersibility in water and stable photoluminescence were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal approach. After tail vein injection of the Gd-doped CDs, they exhibited a relatively long circulation time (∼6 h), enabled efficient passive tumor targeting. Gd-doped CDs accumulate in the kidney and could be cleared out of the body from bladder. Importantly, they exhibited favorable biocompatibility with excellent performance in longitudinal relaxivity rate (r
1 ) of 6.45 mM−1S−1 and radiosensitization enhancements. These results show that Gd-doped CDs are excellent T 1 contrast agents and radiosensitizers, possessing great promise for MRI-guided radiotherapy of tumors.

ce:section.ce:para: Radiotherapy is a mainstay of noninvasive cancer treatment without surgical risks and complications and without the systematic toxicity of chemotherapy [1–3]. Clinically, this technique uses high-energy X-rays or γ-rays to generate toxic photoelectrons and radicals to induce apoptosis of cancerous cells or suppress their proliferation. Despite the fact that radiotherapy is presently the most efficient treatment against solid tumors, it has two urgent limitations that need to be addressed in order to improve the efficacy of cancer therapy [4]. Firstly, more precise localization should be achieved using a variety of techniques and methods to obtain high-definition images. Inaccuracy in localization of radiation therapy could lead to damage of the surrounding normal tissue or insufficient radiation to the tumor [5]. Secondly, improving the sensitivity of tumors to radiotherapy is vital for effective eradication of cancerous cells. Radiation may induce systemic acquired resistance within tumor cells in the hypoxic zone of the solid tumors or trigger underlying molecular repair processes that lead to radioresistance [6]. Therefore, the development of new effective theranostic agents for enhancing resolution of medical imaging and tumor radiosensitivity becomes an urgent priority.

Gadolinium (Gd), a lanthanide element, has been widely used in magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) contrast agents and radiosensitization enhancement due to its unique physical and chemical properties [7–10]. In clinical applications, Gd-chelator complexes possess seven unpaired electrons with a large magnetic moment and are most commonly used as T 1 contrast agents for high-resolution imaging [11]. However, Gd ions released from complexes accumulate in the body and cannot be metabolized. Gd ions can cause considerable biological toxicity by inhibiting calcium channels, and can lead to nephrogenic system fibrosis in patients with renal dysfunction [12]. Recently, research has shown that encapsulating Gd into nano-carriers can effectively suppress Gd release while maintaining the T 1-shortening capacity [13]. Materials used for this strategy include inorganic nanoparticles [14], liposomes [15], micelles [16], and microcapsules [17]. It is worth mentioning that Gd capsulated into carbonaceous dots by simple calcination remains stable with minimal Gd leakage even in harsh biological conditions [18]. Additionally, the high atomic number of Gd (Z = 64) provides dose enhancement during radiation therapy because of the high X-ray photon capture cross-section and Compton scattering effect [19]. Therefore, local deposition of Gd-based radiosensitizers on the tumor site could enhance the effects of X-rays, necessitating a decreased radiation dose and resulting in fewer side effects for normal tissues. Several studies have shown that Gd-based nanoparticles are not only useful as T 1 positive contrast agents for MRI, but also as effective radiosensitizers for tumor treatment [20]. Especially, Motexafin gadolinium (MGd) as a chemotherapeutic drug could deplete the available pool of DNA repair substrates, making them unavailable for repairing the oxidative damage on DNA strands induced by radiation [21]. Despite these advantages, pharmacokinetic limitations of Gd-based compounds include short circulation half-lives and nonspecific distribution, which are the main causes of tumor radiotherapy failure.

Carbon quantum dots (CDs), a new formulation of carbon nanomaterial, have drawn tremendous attention due to their remarkable optical properties, chemical inertness, and biocompatibility [22–24]. Taking advantage of these properties, CDs can be used in a wide range of biomedical applications such as bio-imaging [25], bio-labeling [26], bio-sensing [27], drug delivery [28], gene transfection [29] and photodynamic therapy [30]. Especially, Ge and co-workers reported a novel red-emissive CDs with strong photoacoustic response and high photothermal conversion efficiency (η ≈ 38.5%), which enable the C-dots to act as multifunctional theranostics for simultaneous diagnosis and therapy of cancer [31]. Our previous work demonstrated that doping CDs with heteroatoms effectively tunes their intrinsic properties and introduces beneficial features [32–35]. Based on the results of previous studies, we prepared Gd-doped CDs by simple hydrothermal carbonization of gadopentetic acid (Gd-DTPA) as the gadolinium source material and glycine as the passivation agent. By virtue of the inertness of carbon cages, Gd-doped CDs could minimize Gd leakage even under harsh biological conditions. These specifically designed multifunctional theranostic nanoparticles have great potential as MRI contrast agents with efficient metabolic pathways in vivo and higher longitudinal relaxation efficiency. More importantly, Gd-doped CDs as enhancers of radiosensitivity highlight a novel avenue for overcoming resistance of solid tumors to radiotherapy.

Gadopentetic acid (Gd-DTPA) was purchased from Bayer Pharma AG (Germany). Quinine sulfate (98%, suitable for ﬂuorescence), glycine, and glucose were acquired from Sigma (New York, USA). Ammonia water, NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4, and H2SO4 were obtained from Guangfu Fine Chemical (Tianjin, China). Propidium Iodide (PI), fetal bovine serum (FBS), Dulbecco's Minimum Essential Medium (DMEM) medium were purchased from Invitrogen China limited (China, Shanghai). The Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) was bought from Dojindo (Kumamoto, Japan). All other chemicals were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent (China) unless otherwise noted.

First, 1 g glycine was diluted in 20 mL water, then was mixed with varying amounts of Gd-DTPA (0–2 g) under vigorous stirring to form a transparent homogeneous solution. This solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teﬂon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for different time periods (1–6 h). After cooling to room temperature, the reaction mixture was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 15 min to remove the black precipitates. The brown-yellow supernatant was transferred into a dialysis membrane (MWCO of 1000) and was dialyzed against ultra-pure water for 4 days to remove residues. The dialysis solution was collected and freeze-dried using a vacuum freeze dryer. The Gd-doped CD powders thus obtained were saved for further characterization.

The morphologies of the Gd-doped CDs were examined by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) on a JEM-2100 microscope (Jeol, Japan) under an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. UV-Vis absorption spectra were recorded using a UV-2450 UV-Vis Spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Photoluminescence (PL) emission measurements were made using a Cary Eclipse Fluorometer (Varian, America). The chemical structures of Gd-doped CDs were analyzed using a Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Nicolet Nexus 470, America). The elemental composition of Gd-doped CDs was determined by XPS measurements in a MK II photoelectron spectrometer, using Al-Ka (1486.6 eV) as the X-ray source. The crystal structure of the Gd-doped CDs was characterized via X-Ray diffraction (XRD) patterns on a Rigaku-D/MAX2500 diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan) equipped with Cu Kα (λ = 0.15405 nm) radiation at a scanning speed of 4°/min in the range from 5°to 80°.

The quantum yield (Ф) of the prepared carbon nanodots was determined by a comparative method [36]. Quinine sulfate (reported quantum yield: 54%) was dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4to create a standard solution [refractive index (η) = 1.33]. Aqueous solutions containing varying concentrations of Gd-doped CDs were prepared by dissolving them in distilled water (η = 1.33). Absorbance of the solutions at the excitation wavelength was measured using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of all the samples were recorded using a FLS920 fluorimeter at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The samples were then analyzed using the PL spectrometer and the PL emission intensity of the excitation wavelength at which the CDs and the reference showed the same UV absorbance. The quantum yield was calculated using the following equation:
Φ X
=
Φ
st
(
Grad
X
Grad
st
)
(
η X 2
η
st
2
)

The ST and X denote the standard group and test group, respectively. Φ is the fluorescence quantum yield. Grad is the gradient from the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity vs. absorbance, and η is the refractive index of the solvent. To minimize reabsorption, absorbance in the 10 mm fluorescence cuvette was not allowed to exceed 0.1 at the excitation wavelength.

Hemolysis assay was carried out according to the procedure reported in the literature with slight modification. In brief, fresh mouse blood stabilized with heparin sodium was centrifuged (1, 200 rpm, 15 min), and washed with PBS five times to obtain the mouse red blood cells (MRBCs). Thereafter, the MRBCs were diluted with 0.9 mL water (as positive control), 0.9 mL PBS (as negative control), and 0.9 mL PBS containing Gd-doped CDs with different particle concentrations (50–200 μg/mL), respectively. After 2 h at 37 °C, the absorbance of samples at 541 nm was measured by a UV-2450 UV–Vis spectrophotometer.

Before relaxivity measurement, the gadolinium in the Gd-doped CDs was quantified by inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-MS, Thermal Elemental X7, Thermal Fisher Scientific Inc., USA). The Gd-doped CDs and Magnevist were prepared with concentrations of 0, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8 and 16 mM of total Gd elements in 1% agar-containing solution. The solution were heated and placed into 96-well plate to form gelatinous discs. In vitro MRI of these diluted Gd-doped CDs solutions was performed with Spin Echo sequence. The longitudinal and transverse relaxation times of these discs were measured (at 298 K) and used for calculating the relaxation rate of the samples. T 1-weighted MR images of all the samples were acquired under the following parameters: TR/TE = 476/9.2 m (T 1), 256 × 205 matrices, slice gap = 0.5 mm and thickness = 12 cm.

Animal experiments were executed according to the protocol approved by Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Jiangsu University. Before in vivo experiments, the Gd-doped CDs were filtered through sterilized membrane filters (pore size = 0.22 μm) and stored in sterilized vials for further use. For in vivo MRI studies, transverse images of BALB/c mice were first acquired at different slices to show the heart, kidneys, and bladder planes. The mice were then intravenously injected with 200 μL of Gd-doped CDs solution (10 mg/kg). Time-scale acquisition of post injection images at 10, 30, and 60 min were obtained with the same slices.

The subcutaneous and in situ tumor models were established on four-week-old BALB/c mice. The tumor-bearing mice were injected with Gd-doped CDs (10 mg/kg) via tail vein. The coronal plane images across the tumor were acquired at time points of 15, 30, 60, 120, and 240 min post injection. The images were acquired under the following parameters: TR/TE = 280/300 m, 192 × 123 matrices, slices = 8, thickness = 12 cm, averages = 3, FOV = 8.1 × 6.1.

For histological studies, mice were sacrificed 30 days after intravenous injection of Gd-doped CDs at a dose of 10 mg/kg. Mice without the injection of Gd-doped CDs were used as control groups. Tissues (heart, spleen, liver, lung, and kidney) were harvested from the above two groups (control and test groups). The ex vivo organs were immersed in a buffered solution of 4% paraformaldehyde for 24 h, dehydrated, and treated for inclusion in paraffin. The specimen was sectioned serially at 4 mm thickness and stained with hematoxylin and eosin. The histological sections were observed under an optical microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biodistribution of Gd-doped CDs in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: To study the biodistribution of Gd-doped CDs in vivo, the Gd-doped CDs solution (10 mg/kg) in PBS was injected into BALB/c mice from the tail vein. After 24 h post administration, mice were sacrificed and organs dissected and weighed. For ICP-MS assay, the above each sample was added 5 mL of H2NO3, transferred to flasks, and sealed for predigestion overnight. Then, 3 mL of 30% H2O2 was added to each flask. The flasks were heated at 120 °C for 2 h and then cooled to room temperature. A series of Gd+ standard solutions (0, 0.5, 1.5, 10, 50, and 100 ppb) was prepared with the above solution. Both standard and test solutions were measured by ICP-MS. The amount of Gd elements was finally normalized to the cell number or tissue weight per gram.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of radiosensitizing Gd-doped CDs in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: Clonogenic survival assay was conducted to study the radiosensitization effect in vitro. HepG2 cells were seeded in six-well plates at 4.0 × 104 cells/well and allowed to grow for 16 h. The cells were incubated with Gd-doped CDs diluted in cell culture medium for 6 h. The cells were then irradiated at a series of intensity from 2 Gy to 8 Gy and were allowed to grow for 14 days, fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde at room temperature for 40 min, stained with 1% crystal violet after washing the cells.

Through the MR imaging auxiliary location, therapeutic evaluation of Gd-doped CDs in vivo was verified using the subcutaneous and in situ tumor-bearing ICR mice. When the tumor sizes reached approximately 100 mm3, the radiotherapy was conducted at 3 Gy radiation dose per fraction at a total dose of 9 Gy within one week, 3 h after the intravenous injection of Gd-doped CDs (10 mg/kg), on a Siemens Primus clinical linear accelerator (6 MeV) using a 1.5 cm × 3 cm radiation field to cover the entire tumor. Tumor growth and mouse weight were measured in the following days. Tumor sizes were measured by a caliper and calculated as follows: V = ab2/2. Where V (mm3) is the volume of the tumor, and a (mm) and b (mm) are the tumor length and tumor width, respectively. The relative tumour volumes were normalized to their initial sizes. Each group was conducted on sextuplet mice, wherein statistical analysis was performed using Student's two-tailed t-test (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.001).

The phase structure of Gd-doped CDs was further investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern using pristine graphite and graphite oxide (GO) powders as comparison groups. As shown in Fig. 1C, one main diffraction peak (2θ = 12.04°) and two minor peaks (2θ = 29.6°, 42.1°) can be observed in the diffraction pattern of Gd-doped CDs, which correspond to the characteristic peaks of graphite (002 plane) and GO (100 plane), respectively. The main lattice spacing of the Gd-doped CDs was smaller (0.73 nm calculated by the Bragg formula) than the spacing of GO (d001 = 0.85 nm), and the upward shift in peak position may be attributed to a decrease in the spacing between sp3 layers. Compared to the ordered crystal structure of graphite (002 planes, 2θ = 26.5°), the broad diffraction peak of Gd-doped CDs around 29.6° (d = 0.33 nm) was attributed to highly disordered carbon and a decrease in the sp2 (CC) layer spacing during the carbonization process. The shoulder peak at 2θ = 42.5° (d = 0.212 nm) is ascribed to in-plane diffraction from the graphene-like structure of the Gd-doped CDs, which corresponds to the {100} and {101} plane of graphite. The decrease in intensity of the three peaks and the increase of the full width half maximum (FWHM) are due to the amorphous carbon structure of the prepared Gd-doped CDs. These results indicate that Gd-doped CDs with poor crystalline nature possessed a heterogeneous multi-layered structure, which is consistent with many other reports on properties of CDs.

The chemical structure and composition of the Gd-doped CDs was investigated using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) patterns. FTIR spectroscopy was conducted for both naked and polyacrylic acid (PAA)-coated samples. As shown in Fig. 1D, the FTIR spectra exhibited characteristic absorption bands of OH and NH stretching vibrations of amine groups at 3353 cm−1, CH stretching vibrations at 2927 cm−1, CO stretching vibrations at 1608 cm−1,CC stretching vibrations at 1413 cm−1, CN stretching vibrations at 1382 cm−1 and CH stretching vibrations at 1068 cm−1. The XPS survey spectrum of the as-produced Gd-doped CDs (Fig. 2 A) showed two typical binding energies at 1187 and 1219 eV, corresponding to a spin-orbit coupled Gd (3d5/2) and Gd (3d3/2), respectively. The peak positions are in agreement with the energy level reported for Gd3+ in Gd2O3. The high-resolution spectrum of Gd4d revealed the presence of two peaks centered at 147.2 eV and 141.4 eV, which was similar to the spectral patterns of Gd (2p3/2) and Gd (2p5/2) of Gd-DTPA. This result indicated that the partial Gd3+ coordination and the chemical environment do not change signiﬁcantly after carbonization. The three strong photoelectron binding energies at 284, 398, and 530 eV corresponded to the characteristic peaks of C1s, N1s and O1s respectively, which indicated that the Gd-doped CDs were mainly composed of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen atoms. The high–resolution spectrum of C1S (Fig. 2C) indicated the three main peaks at 284.6, 285 and 287 eV, which were attributed to CC, CN and CO, CO respectively. Moreover, the spectrum of O1s (Fig. 2D) further confirmed these observations with two characteristic oxygen states of CO (531.2eV) and CO (530.2eV). The elemental analysis experiment demonstrated that the nitrogen content was approximately 7.34%, carbon content was approximately 55.32%, hydrogen content was approximately 3.62%, and oxygen content was approximately 66.72% (calculated). As shown from FTIR and XPS, we conclude that there are various functional groups located at the surface, such as amino, carbonyl, carboxylate, and hydroxyl groups, which endow the Gd-doped CDs with good water solubility and improved fluorescent properties. Considering these results, we propose that Gd atoms as inorganic and organic chemical formula may incorporate into the surface and internal of carbonaceous nanoparticles.

The optical properties of the Gd-doped CDs were characterized with UV/Vis and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. The absorption spectrum (Fig. 3 A) shows no obvious absorption peak in an aqueous solution of Gd-doped CDs. The aqueous solution was pale yellow and transparent in daylight, but was bright blue under UV excitation (inset, Fig. 3A). The quantum yield of this solution, measured using quinine bisulfate as a standard, was 13.4%. As shown in Fig. 2C, the Gd-doped CDs displayed a broad range of emission wavelengths (preeminent multicolor fluorescent emission) and excitation-dependent emission, which was well correlated with the conventional CDs. The maximum emission at 475 nm was observed with 370 nm excitation. In addition, the aqueous solution of CDs was a long-term homogeneous phase without any noticeable precipitation at room temperature. Furthermore, the Tyndall effect was clearly observed when a beam of red light was used to illuminate the aqueous solution of Gd-doped CDs (Fig. 3B). The overall results illustrate that the prepared Gd-doped CDs exhibit favorable fluorescent properties and high dispersibility in an aqueous solution.

Biocompatibility of Gd-doped CDs is critical for their use as theranostic agents. In order to evaluate the cytotoxicity in vitro, the effects of Gd-doped CDs treatment was assessed in human lung adenocarcinoma HepG2 cells and VSMCs with a MTS assay. Fig. 4 A shows that Gd-doped CDs exhibited extremely low cytotoxicity in both cell lines, with cell viability at approximately 90% even at a Gd-doped CD concentration of 200 μg/mL and a 48 h exposure time. In addition, the bright-field optical images also showed no morphological change after Gd-doped CD treatment, suggesting the Gd-doped CDs had good biocompatibility as prepared. The uptake of the Gd-doped CDs in the living cells was evaluated using the inherent fluorescent property. After incubation with Gd-doped CDs for 6 h, the HepG2 and VSMC cells were washed to remove extracellular Gd-doped CDs. The labeled cells appeared bright green fluorescence emitted from Gd-doped CDs distributed in the cytosol (Fig. 4B). The Gd-doped CDs were clearly observed in cell membranes and cytoplasmic regions, especially around the nucleus, while the photoluminescence of Gd-doped CDs was very weak in the cell nucleus. These results demonstrated that a certain amount of Gd-doped CDs could be readily internalized into the cells and dispersed into the cytoplasm in a short time. These results led us to the conclusion that our Gd-doped CDs possessed low cytotoxicity in cells, which agreed well with the findings of previous studies on the interaction of living cells with other CDs [37].

Furthermore, the hemocompatibility in vivo was estimated using a hemolysis assay. As shown in Fig. 4C, we observed hemolysis of red blood cells in the positive control. On the contrary, no obvious hemolysis was observed after 2 h incubation with concentrations of Gd-doped CDs ranging from 0 to 200 μg/mL, which was similar to the saline negative control. Compared to the negative control, the percentage of hemolysis at different concentrations of Gd-doped CDs was quantitated based on the absorbance the supernatant at 541 nm. The results indicated that the percentages of hemolysis in the samples treated with Gd-doped CDs were all less than 3% in the studied concentration range, which verified their favorable hemocompatibility. These results clearly suggested that the prepared Gd-doped CDs had very low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility.

MR contrast ability of Gd-doped CDs was investigated in vitro alongside the commercial contrast agent Magnevist (Gd-DTPA) as a control, using PBS as the solvent (pH. = 7.4, 0.2 M). T 1-weighted MR phantom images of both contrast agents were conducted in a 3 T MRI scanner. With an increase in Gd concentration, the signal intensity of T 1-weight phantom images increased, indicating that all samples have the potential to enhance MRI contrast on T 1-weighted sequences (Fig. 5 A). Furthermore, plots of signal intensity versus inversion time give the T 1-relaxation times of each contrast agent at certain concentrations. As shown in Fig. 5B, r 1 value of Gd-doped CDs was measured to be 6.45 mM−1s−1, which was signiﬁcantly higher than the r1 of Magnevist (4.05 M−1s−1) under the same conditions. The enhanced r 1 was mainly attributed to the much higher hydrodynamic radius and surface area of Gd-doped CDs nanoparticles compared to Magnevist molecules, therefore, more Gd ions doped in the lattice of Gd-doped CDs became accessible to water molecules, helping to shorten the longitudinal relaxation and enhance the r 1 value.

Gd-doped CDs were intravenously injected into the mice, and T 1-weighted images were acquired at 0 min and 10 min, 3 h and 6 h, respectively, using Magnevist for comparison. As shown in Fig. 6 A, brighter regions of kidney, bladder, and heart as indicated by the red arrows can be clearly observed 10 min post-injection, demonstrating that the Gd-doped CDs can serve as a contrast agent like Magnevist and can enhance T 1 relaxation in these major organs in vivo. Importantly, the contrast enhancement of Gd-doped CDs was maintained for 6 h post-injection, while the enhancement of Magnevist almost disappeared, indicating Gd-doped CDs possess longer retention time in vivo. The signal of Gd-doped CDs in the bladder continued to increase over time, while the signal of Magnevist disappeared 6 h post-injection. Interestingly, the signal change of Gd-doped CDs in the heart was found to be subtle throughout the course of the experiment, whereas no change occurred in the Magnevist signal. For quantitative analysis of the MR contrast effect, we calculated the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) by finely analyzing regions of interest (ROIs) of the MRI images and calculated the values of SNRpost/SNRpre to represent the relative signal enhancement (RSE). The RSE of Gd-doped CDs in the kidney were 1.48, 1.46, 1.31, and the corresponding Magnevist were just 1.38, 1.16, 0.96 at 10 min, 1 h, 6 h post-administration respectively (Fig. 6B). Similar to this trend, the RSE of Gd-doped CDs in the bladder (2.47, 2.43, 2.98) was stronger than Magnevist (1.8, 2.49, 1.18). It is notable that Gd-doped CDs maintain high RSE in the major organs 6 h after administration, while RSE of Magnevist has nearly subsided to pre-injection levels by this time. We calculated the half-life of Gd-doped CDs was about 2.5 h in mice (Fig. S2), which was much longer than that of Gd complex small molecules, with a high excretion rate (half-life of about several minutes in small animals). Compared with Magnevist, Gd-doped CDs displayed a longer circulation time, possessed efficient renal clearance, and finally was excreted from the body by urine, which was consistent with previously reported findings [38,39]. Moreover, the prolonged circulation time in vivo may increase the possibility of passive tumor targeting by an enhanced permeation and retention (EPR) effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MR imaging of tumors in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: Gd-doped CDs with excellent MR imaging performance and long circulation time provide a great opportunity for imaging tumors through an EPR effect. We thus established subcutaneous and in situ liver cancer models to investigate if hepatocellular carcinomas (HCCs) can be detected by Gd-doped CD-enhanced MRI. After intravenous injection of the Gd-doped CDs, we observed that the subcutaneous tumor region become brighter than the surrounding tissue as seen by both coronal and transverse scanning (Fig. 7 A). As shown in Fig. S4, signal intensity in tumor region did not change obviously compared with the surrounding normal tissues after injection of Magnevist. However, the signal enhancement in both the tumor tissue and normal liver region can be found after administration of Gd-doped CDs. The RES of the subcutaneous tumor dramatically reached 1.93 and 1.74 with coronal and transverse scanning, respectively, indicating the effective accumulation of the Gd-doped CDs in the tumor region through the EPR effect. On the contrary, we observed the “washout” appearance in tumor regions in situ, where the RES of the tumor in situ decreased to 0.74 and 0.77 with the coronal and transverse scanning (Fig. 7B). The washout appearance is well defined as a visually assessed temporal reduction in enhancement relative to surrounding liver tissue from an earlier to a later phase, resulting in portal venous or delayed phase hypoenhancement. To confirm the diagnostic accuracy, mice were sacrificed and the target tissue regions were excised, fixed, sliced, and stained with H&E and anti-Ki67, a marker for cancer cells. As illustrated in Fig. 8 A, the H&E and immunohistochemistry images showed the presence of pathological tissue changes and hepatocellular carcinoma in both the subcutaneous tumor samples and the liver, which further verified the accuracy of detection of Gd-doped CDs-enhanced MRI diagnosis. These findings demonstrated that Gd-doped CDs are highly sensitive for MRI visualization of tumors. Many studies showed that the long circulation time could promote the efficiency of passive targeting through leaky vasculature of solid tumors.

Understanding the biodistribution behavior of the Gd-doped CDs into the major organs is crucial for their further in vivo biomedical applications. In order to understand the metabolism and distribution of our novel particles in vivo, the circulation fate of Gd-doped CDs was investigated with a ICP-MS method using BALB/c mice as model (Fig. 8B). After 24 h post-injection, spleen and heart have a significant uptake of the particles, with an uptake of 0.129 g/Kg in the spleen and 0.032 g/Kg in the heart. The ultra-small size of the inorganic nanoparticles seemed to allow a portion of the particles to escape from the recognition of reticuloendothelial system (RES) and accumulate in the other organs such as 0.006 g/Kg in the liver, 0.017 g/Kg in the lung, and 0.015 g/Kg in the kidney. The distributional difference may be attributed to abundant blood supply in the heart and presence of RES in the spleen. However, the MRI enhancement of spleen was not observed, which may be ascribed to different anatomical structure. In other words, the center of the anatomical structures spleen and liver are not in same plane. Furthermore, the histological assessment of the harvested susceptible organs (liver, heart, spleen, lung, and kidney) showed that the Gd-doped CDs did not cause obvious adverse effects, such as tissue damage, inflammation, or lesions when compared to the control group after 7 days post-injection (Fig. 8C).

Circulation time in vivo are crucial for EPR-driven tumor accumulation because nanoparticles maintained a relatively high concentration could be readily uptaken by targeted tissues. Therefore, the blood clearance was investigated via ICP-MS and terminal half-life was then calculated based on a one-compartment pharmacokinetic model. It was determined that the circulation half-life of Gd-doped CDs is ∼2 h during the early distribution phase and 12 h during the elimination phase (Fig. S2). These preliminary results showed that the Gd-doped CDs exhibit predictable biodistribution and favorable biocompatibility during long-term retention in the body.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Radiosensitization assessment in vitro and in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by the excellent biocompatibility and high longitudinal relaxation efficiency, we then investigated whether the Gd-doped CDs as radiosensitizers could enhance the therapeutic effectiveness of radiotherapy. Firstly, the proliferation profiles of HepG2 cells after treatment with X-rays and Gd-doped CDs were studied using a clonogenic assay. As depicted in Fig. S3, no significant difference in survival fraction was found in Gd-doped CDs only group, while X-ray treatment decreased HepG2 cell colony formation. Notably, the combination of X-rays and Gd-doped CDs significantly inhibited colony formation, indicating the Gd-doped CDs enhanced the radiation dose. This enhancement effect was further investigated using a series of radiation doses and concentrations of the Gd-doped CDs. It was worth noting that single Gd-doped CDs treatment at high concentration (200 μg/mL) really reduced the growth of HepG2 cells to 90.1%. With increasing doses of radiation and Gd-doped CDs, the survival fraction of HepG2 cells dramatically decreased to 64.9% during treatment with 8Gy radiation and 200 μg/mL of Gd-doped CDs. Based on previous reports [[40] [41]], this enhancement of mediated-gadolinium radiosensitization may be contributed to photoelectrons and Auger electrons generated from the X-ray irradiated nanoparticles. Energy deposition by electrons will hydrolyze water molecules surrounding cells and their nuclei, producing free radicals that will induce DNA damage, eventually leading to cell death. In this study, our results strongly demonstrated the high synergistic inhibition efficiency of the Gd-doped CDs for tumor radiotherapy.

To preliminarily verify the feasibility of imaging-guided radiotherapy in vivo, mice bearing 7–8 mm subcutaneous Herps flank tumors were established and divided into four groups (n = 8 per groups). Mice were monitored for tumor growth and were sacrificed when the tumors reached the predetermined threshold volume (1300 mm3). According to the MR imaging results, the signal intensity of tumor lesion was markedly enhanced contrast after intravenous injection of Gd-doped CDs. Consequently, the radiation process could be guided by MR imaging, where the X-ray irradiation could be localized on the tumor site as much as possible, assisted by real-time monitoring with MR imaging.

On the one hand, the tumor growth curve showed tumor volume increased rapidly both in control and single Gd-doped CDs groups during the entire period of investigation, enlarging nearly 3-fold in 7 days (Fig. 9 A). In the group treated with X-ray irradiation alone, tumor growth was inhibited during this process, as saw by a decrease in tumor volume of approximately 12%. In contrast, the Gd-doped CDs treatment combined with radiation group showed that the tumors shrank by a maximum volume percentage of 53%. Compared with radiotherapy alone, the introduction of the Gd-doped CDs during the radiation process caused a remarkable dose enhancement effect to inhibit the growth of solid tumors. On the other hand, the survival time was measured from the time of radiation and processed using Kaplan-Meier survival analysis (Fig. 9B). Mice that received Gd-doped CDs prior to radiation therapy exhibited a statistically significant (p < 0.05) improvement in median survival time (MST = 32 days), which corresponded to an increase in lifespan (ILS) of 133%, compared to mice treated with radiation alone (MST = 24 days). The efficiency of this stereoscopic conformal radiotherapy may be attributed to precise localization guided by MR imaging and enhancement of mediated-gadolinium radiosensitization. These results indicate that the EPR-dependent accumulation of Gd-doped CDs within tumors can guide and enhance the efficacy of radiation therapy.

In summary, we have successfully presented a precise theranostic nanoplatform based on Gd-doped CDs designed specifically for MRI-guided radiotherapy. The newly developed Gd-doped CDs with high longitudinal relaxation efficiency and long circulation time could provide better anatomical and pathophysiologic detection of tumors, target delineation, and precise positioning for accurate MRI-guided radiotherapy. More importantly, the constitutive heavy elements in this novel agent have been demonstrated to be capable of enhancing irradiation dose deposition by a radiosensitizing effect. The preliminary results from tumor xenografts showed that the dose enhancement effect observed in vivo could be readily translated to favorable tumor control by the Gd-doped CDs radiosensitized radiotherapy. Thus, the present results demonstrate that the Gd-doped CDs could be developed as a highly promising theranostic agent for both MRI and enhancement of radiosensitivity, which opens up more opportunities for future clinical cancer therapy.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 81301316, 31200676), Ph.D. Programs Foundation of Ministry of Education of China (No. 20123227120008), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2013M540425, 2014T70487, 2015M571705), Senior Talents Scientific Research Foundation of Jiangsu University (No. 13JDG022, 11JDG113), and Health and family planning commission of jiangsu province scientific research subject (H201557).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.biomaterials.2016.07.008.
